title: Rootless Container Port Publishing via 127.0.0.1 Issue
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug


**Description**

When trying to publish a port on a rootless container via 127.0.0.1, that port binding crashes when other containers try and access that port.

For example, I am using a postgres container to publish 127.0.0.1:5432, and then literally trying to curl 127.0.0.1:5432, when this happens, podman port -l no longer lists the port binding. This only happens when trying to curl this port from another container. If I try and curl 127.0.0.1:5432 via the host OS, it works fine.

When the podman port -l no longer lists that port, the container is still running, not in a crashed state. It can even still access the internet, its just no longer publishing its port.

https://paste.centos.org/view/5e3e7f59

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `/usr/bin/podman run -dt -p 127.0.0.1:5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password -e POSTGRES_USER=mattermost -e POSTGRES_DB=mattermost   docker.io/postgres:12`

2. `podman port -l`
Notice the port binding exists

3. `curl 127.0.0.1:5432`
Notice we can curl that port from the host.

4. `podman run -it docker.io/mattermost/mattermost-team-edition curl 127.0.0.1:5432`
Notice we got an error as if that port doesn't exist

5. `podman port -l`
Notice that port mapping no longer exists.

**Describe the results you received:**
`podman port -l` no longer lists the port mapping and I cannot access that port from another container.

**Describe the results you expected:**
Containers should be able to access the host's localhost for rootless container communication.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.8.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.13.6
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


comments.body: All the subsystems are new to me, so I could be guessing completely wrong. Does this mean that slirp4netns is "failing silently" and it is responsible for that forwarding of the port?

Per debugging yesterday, I suspect that slirp is still up, but the rootlesskit port forwarder is dying.

@giuseppe @AkihiroSuda Mind taking a look at this one?
Please provide the full reproducer. Both command 1 and 3 don't seem correct.

> 
> 
> Please provide the full reproducer. Both command 1 and 3 don't seem correct.

I apologize, I forgot I use an env-file for the postgres stuff. I have edited the original post.

> When trying to publish a port on a rootless container via 127.0.0.1, that port binding crashes when other containers try and access that port.

The ~~port binding seems crashing~~ (**EDIT**: port binding itself is alive but `podman port -l` CLI doesn't show it up)  just when yet another container started up.
So command 4 can be just like `podman run -it --rm alpine`. And anyway you can't connect 127.0.0.1 from  a container (regardless to rootless or rootful) unless you specify `--net=host`.
The port itself seems alive, but `podman port -l` CLI seems broken.

With that in mind, what is the preferred method for communicating between two containers via localhost so that traffic doesn't have to "leave the host" or open up any ports on a network-facing IP?

We were originally putting them in the same pod, but we were seeing that if we restart a container within that pod, it cannot re-bind to a published port unless we restart the _entire_ pod, which is not ideal.

> I suggest using UNIX sockets

Could you elaborate further? Some of the things you'd mentioned are not extremely obvious (e.g. the `--net=host` thing I'd not even spotted until you'd mentioned it).

See https://docs.mattermost.com/install/sockets-db.html . You will need to create shared volume for `/run`.

> the --net=host thing 

In Rootless Podman, each of containers has its own network namespace. i.e. each of containers have  its own "localhost" which is different from "the real localhost". 
`podman run --net=host` disables network namespace so that the container can access "the real localhost" as "localhost".
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think this can be closed as we've posted #5828.
